labs.title: Network Interface Inspection with ifconfig
labs.concept: Inspecting network interfaces using ifconfig on a remote Linux VM
instructions: SSH into your Azure VM (VM-Debian-1)
Run: sudo ifconfig
Identify the eth0 interface and note the following:
- IPv4 address (inet), netmask, and broadcast address
- IPv6 address (inet6) and prefix length
- MAC address (ether)
- MTU value
- RX/TX packet counts and byte counts
Identify the lo (loopback) interface and note:
- IPv4 address (127.0.0.1) and netmask (255.0.0.0)
- IPv6 address (::1) and prefix length (128)
- MTU value (65536)
Compare the eth0 flags (UP, BROADCAST, RUNNING, MULTICAST) with the lo flags (UP, LOOPBACK, RUNNING)
Run: ip a — compare the output with ifconfig
Run: ip -4 addr show eth0 — to see only IPv4 on eth0
Run: ip -6 addr show eth0 — to see only IPv6 on eth0
Run: ifconfig eth0 — to see only the eth0 interface
Note: ifconfig is from net-tools (legacy), ip is from iproute2 (modern)

labs.title: Azure VM Network Configuration
labs.concept: Understanding network configuration on an Azure Debian VM
instructions: SSH into your Azure VM
Run: sudo ifconfig eth0
Note the private IP (10.0.0.4) — this is the Azure VNet internal address
Run: ip route show — find the default gateway
Run: curl ifconfig.me — find your public IP (different from private IP)
Explain: Why is the private IP (10.0.0.4) different from the public IP?
Run: cat /etc/resolv.conf — check DNS configuration
Run: nslookup google.com — test DNS resolution
Run: traceroute google.com — see the network path (install with: sudo apt install traceroute)
Note the broadcast address (10.0.0.255) and netmask (255.255.255.0) — this is a /24 subnet

labs.title: ifconfig vs ip Command Comparison
labs.concept: Comparing legacy net-tools (ifconfig) with modern iproute2 (ip) commands
instructions: SSH into your Azure VM
Run both commands side by side and compare output:
ifconfig           vs   ip a
ifconfig eth0      vs   ip addr show eth0
ifconfig -a        vs   ip link show
route -n           vs   ip route show
netstat -tulpn     vs   ss -tulpn
arp -a             vs   ip neigh show
Note which package provides each set:
ifconfig, route, netstat, arp → net-tools (legacy, may not be installed)
ip, ss → iproute2 (modern, always available)
If ifconfig is not found, install it: sudo apt install net-tools
Document 3 differences you notice between the outputs

labs.title: RX/TX Packet Analysis
labs.concept: Understanding receive/transmit statistics on network interfaces
instructions: SSH into your Azure VM
Run: sudo ifconfig eth0
Find the RX (receive) and TX (transmit) lines:
RX packets, bytes, errors, dropped, overruns, frame
TX packets, bytes, errors, dropped, overruns, carrier, collisions
Run: ping -c 100 google.com &
Wait 10 seconds, then run ifconfig again
Compare the RX/TX packet counts — they should have increased
Run: ip -s link show eth0 — another way to see interface statistics
Run: cat /proc/net/dev — raw kernel interface stats
Calculate: How many MB were received? (bytes / 1024 / 1024)
Check for errors: Are there any dropped packets or errors? What would that mean?
